title: Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI): Architectures, Taxonomies, and Evaluation of Large Language Model Agents
author: V, Arunkumar, R., Gangadharan G., Buyya, Rajkumar
summary: This arXiv paper presents a comprehensive framework for understanding agentic AI systems built on large language models. The authors propose a unified taxonomy decomposing agents into six core components: Perception, Brain, Planning, Action, Tool Use, and Collaboration. The work traces the evolution from passive text-generation models to autonomous systems capable of reasoning, planning, and acting in complex environments—spanning software engineering, scientific discovery, and robotics. Beyond architectural analysis, the paper surveys evaluation methodologies and identifies critical open challenges including hallucination in action execution, infinite loops, and prompt injection vulnerabilities. This is essential reading for engineers and researchers building or deploying LLM-based agent systems, offering both conceptual grounding and practical insights into emerging design patterns and standards like Model Context Protocol (MCP).
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2601.12560 (cs) [Submitted on 18 Jan 2026] Title: Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI): Architectures, Taxonomies, and Evaluation of Large Language Model Agents Authors: Arunkumar V , Gangadharan G.R. , Rajkumar Buyya View a PDF of the paper titled Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI): Architectures, Taxonomies, and Evaluation of Large Language Model Agents, by Arunkumar V and 2 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Artificial Intelligence is moving from models that only generate text to Agentic AI, where systems behave as autonomous entities that can perceive, reason, plan, and act. Large Language Models (LLMs) are no longer used only as passive knowledge engines but as cognitive controllers that combine memory, tool use, and feedback from their environment to pursue extended goals. This shift already supports the automation of complex workflows in software engineering, scientific discovery, and web navigation, yet the variety of emerging designs, from simple single loop agents to hierarchical multi agent systems, makes the landscape hard to navigate. In this paper, we investigate architectures and propose a unified taxonomy that breaks agents into Perception, Brain, Planning, Action, Tool Use, and Collaboration. We use this lens to describe the move from linear reasoning procedures to native inference time reasoning models, and the transition from fixed API calls to open standards like the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Native Computer Use. We also group the environments in which these agents operate, including digital operating systems, embodied robotics, and other specialized domains, and we review current evaluation practices. Finally, we highlight open challenges, such as hallucination in action, infinite loops, and prompt injection, and outline future research directions toward more robust and reliable autonomous systems. Comments: 28 pages, 4 figures, 5 tables Subjects: Artificial Intelligence (cs.AI) ; Multiagent Systems (cs.MA) Cite as: arXiv:2601.12560 [cs.AI] (or arXiv:2601.12560v1 [cs.AI] for this version) https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2601.12560 Focus to learn more arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite Submission history From: Gangadharan G R [ view email ] [v1] Sun, 18 Jan 2026 19:51:16 UTC (1,304 KB) Full-text links: Access Paper: View a PDF of the paper titled Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI): Architectures, Taxonomies, and Evaluation of Large Language Model Agents, by Arunkumar V and 2 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) TeX Source view license Current browse context: cs.AI < prev | next > new | recent | 2026-01 Change to browse by: cs cs.MA References & Citations NASA ADS Google Scholar Semantic Scholar export BibTeX citation Loading... takeaways: Agents decompose into six architectural components: Perception, Brain, Planning, Action, Tool Use, Collaboration—enabling systematic comparison across designs.
Evolution from linear reasoning to native inference-time reasoning models and fixed APIs to open standards (MCP, Native Computer Use) reshapes agent capabilities.
Key open challenges: hallucination during action execution, infinite loop prevention, and prompt injection attacks require focused research for robust deployment.
Agent evaluation practices remain nascent; standardized benchmarks across digital OS, embodied robotics, and specialized domains are critical.
Hierarchical multi-agent systems represent a design frontier beyond simple single-loop agents for automating complex workflows.

title: Orchestral AI: A Framework for Agent Orchestration
author: Roman, Alexander, Roman, Jacob
summary: Orchestral is a lightweight Python framework that addresses the fragmentation problem in LLM agent development by providing a unified, type-safe interface for building agents across multiple LLM providers. The paper tackles vendor lock-in and API incompatibility by introducing a single universal representation for messages, tools, and LLM usage, eliminating manual format translation and reducing framework complexity. Key innovations include automatic tool schema generation from Python type hints, synchronous execution with streaming support for deterministic behavior, and a modular architecture supporting advanced capabilities like tool calling, context compaction, workspace sandboxing, and MCP integration—making it valuable for developers building portable, reproducible agent systems across heterogeneous LLM providers.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2601.02577 (cs) [Submitted on 5 Jan 2026] Title: Orchestral AI: A Framework for Agent Orchestration Authors: Alexander Roman , Jacob Roman View a PDF of the paper titled Orchestral AI: A Framework for Agent Orchestration, by Alexander Roman and 1 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: The rapid proliferation of LLM agent frameworks has forced developers to choose between vendor lock-in through provider-specific SDKs and complex multi-package ecosystems that obscure control flow and hinder reproducibility. Integrating tool calling across multiple LLM providers remains a core engineering challenge due to fragmented APIs, incompatible message formats, and inconsistent streaming and tool-calling behavior, making it difficult to build portable, reliable agent systems. We introduce Orchestral, a lightweight Python framework that provides a unified, type-safe interface for building LLM agents across major providers while preserving the simplicity required for scientific computing and production deployment. Orchestral defines a single universal representation for messages, tools, and LLM usage that operates seamlessly across providers, eliminating manual format translation and reducing framework-induced complexity. Automatic tool schema generation from Python type hints removes the need for handwritten descriptors while maintaining type safety across provider boundaries. A synchronous execution model with streaming support enables deterministic behavior, straightforward debugging, and real-time interaction without introducing server dependencies. The framework's modular architecture cleanly separates provider integration, tool execution, conversation orchestration, and user-facing interfaces, enabling extensibility without architectural entanglement. Orchestral supports advanced agent capabilities found in larger frameworks, including rich tool calling, context compaction, workspace sandboxing, user approval workflows, sub-agents, memory management, and MCP integration. Comments: 17 pages, 3 figures. For more information visit this https URL Subjects: Artificial Intelligence (cs.AI) ; Instrumentation and Methods for Astrophysics (astro-ph.IM); High Energy Physics - Phenomenology (hep-ph) ACM classes: I.2.0 Cite as: arXiv:2601.02577 [cs.AI] (or arXiv:2601.02577v1 [cs.AI] for this version) https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2601.02577 Focus to learn more arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite Submission history From: Alexander Roman [ view email ] [v1] Mon, 5 Jan 2026 22:02:11 UTC (1,694 KB) Full-text links: Access Paper: View a PDF of the paper titled Orchestral AI: A Framework for Agent Orchestration, by Alexander Roman and 1 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) TeX Source view license Current browse context: cs.AI < prev | next > new | recent | 2026-01 Change to browse by: astro-ph astro-ph.IM cs hep-ph References & Citations INSPIRE HEP NASA ADS Google Scholar Semantic Scholar export BibTeX citation Loading... takeaways: Unified interface eliminates vendor lock-in and API fragmentation across major LLM providers with type-safe message/tool representations
Automatic tool schema generation from Python type hints removes handwritten descriptors while maintaining cross-provider type safety
Synchronous execution model with streaming enables deterministic debugging and real-time interaction without server dependencies
Modular architecture cleanly separates provider integration, tool execution, and orchestration for extensibility without architectural entanglement
Supports advanced agent capabilities: tool calling, context compaction, sandboxing, approval workflows, sub-agents, memory management, and MCP integration

title: Daily Papers - Hugging Face
summary: AdaptOrch presents a formal framework for dynamically orchestrating multiple LLM agents across different topologies (parallel, sequential, hierarchical, hybrid) based on task characteristics. As LLM performance across providers converges, the paper argues that multi-agent orchestration topology selection has become more important than individual model capability. The framework contributes a Performance Convergence Scaling Law, a polynomial-time Topology Routing Algorithm, and an Adaptive Synthesis Protocol with theoretical guarantees. Validation on coding, reasoning, and RAG tasks shows 12-23% improvements over static baselines using identical models, positioning orchestration design as an independent optimization lever distinct from model scaling.
content: 2 AdaptOrch: Task-Adaptive Multi-Agent Orchestration in the Era of LLM Performance Convergence As large language models from diverse providers converge toward comparable benchmark performance, the traditional paradigm of selecting a single best model per task yields diminishing returns. We argue that orchestration topology -- the structural composition of how multiple agents are coordinated, parallelized, and synthesized -- now dominates system-level performance over individual model capability. We present AdaptOrch, a formal framework for task-adaptive multi-agent orchestration that dynamically selects among four canonical topologies (parallel, sequential, hierarchical, and hybrid) based on task dependency graphs and empirically derived domain characteristics. Our framework introduces three key contributions: (1) a Performance Convergence Scaling Law, formalizing conditions under which orchestration selection outweighs model selection; (2) a Topology Routing Algorithm that maps task decomposition DAGs to optimal orchestration patterns in O(|V| + |E|) time; and (3) an Adaptive Synthesis Protocol with provable termination guarantees and heuristic consistency scoring for parallel agent outputs. We validate AdaptOrch across coding (SWE-bench), reasoning (GPQA), and retrieval-augmented generation tasks, demonstrating that topology-aware orchestration achieves 12-23% improvement over static single-topology baselines, even when using identical underlying models. Our results establish orchestration design as a first-class optimization target independent of model scaling. 1 authors · Feb 18 1
takeaways: Orchestration topology now dominates performance when LLM benchmarks converge across providers
Dynamic topology selection (parallel/sequential/hierarchical/hybrid) improves results 12-23% over static approaches
Topology routing maps task DAGs to optimal patterns in O(|V|+|E|) time with formal complexity guarantees
Performance Convergence Scaling Law formalizes when orchestration outweighs model selection as optimization target

title: AI Trends 2026: Test-Time Reasoning and the Rise of Reflective Agents
summary: This article surveys key AI trends for 2026, focusing on test-time reasoning, reinforcement learning for strategic tool use, and evolving agent architectures. The main argument is that AI systems are shifting from static inference to dynamic reasoning—allocating more compute at inference time, using RL to teach models when and how to invoke tools, and adopting cleaner agent designs with memory and self-critique. For technical readers, this matters because it explains the architectural and training shifts reshaping LLM-based applications: systems are becoming more reflective, transparent about limitations, and capable of multi-step problem-solving without requiring larger models or retraining.
content: Back to Articles AI Trends 2026: Test-Time Reasoning and the Rise of Reflective Agents Community Article Published December 7, 2025 Upvote 3 Ivan aufklarer Follow More Compute at Test Time (Better Reasoning on Demand) RL Makes AI Think (and Use Tools) Strategically Evolving Agent Architectures (Memory, Multi-Agent & Self-Critique) Standard Toolkits: MCP and Agent SDKs Context Engineering & Long-Term Memory Multimodal Models (Vision-Language Fusion) Conclusion: What to Expect in 2026 In 2025, AI systems – especially large language models (LLMs) – are evolving beyond basic text completion. We’re seeing smarter ways for models to reason, use tools, remember context, and handle multiple data types. Below is a concise look at key trends, in a slightly informal tone, highlighting what’s new and likely going mainstream as we head into 2026. More Compute at Test Time (Better Reasoning on Demand) One big shift is allocating more compute during inference (when the model is answering) – essentially letting the AI “think harder” on tough problems. As I covered in the blog post , this test-time compute (a.k.a. inference-time compute scaling) means giving models extra cycles or steps after training to boost performance. Instead of one quick pass to get an answer, the model can take multiple passes or run more elaborate internal reasoning. For example, Anthropic’s Claude offers an extended thinking mode that branches out into a longer reasoning process for complex queries. This can be done serially or in parallel before converging on a final answer. Open research efforts like DeepSeek-R1 exemplify this trend, using extensive multi-step deliberation at inference to significantly improve accuracy. The payoff is better reasoning and fewer mistakes – some models even achieve “superhuman” problem-solving on certain benchmarks – but with the obvious trade-off of higher latency and cost for those extra compute cycles. It’s a classic quality vs. speed trade. We’ve essentially entered an era where, rather than just making models bigger, we make them think more when needed. This approach is already creeping into user-facing apps (e.g. premium chatbot tiers that offer a “deep reasoning” option), and it’s likely to become a standard feature in 2026. RL Makes AI Think (and Use Tools) Strategically Another trend is using reinforcement learning (RL) to train models to be more reflective and strategic – especially in how they use tools. Beyond just RLHF for nice answers, new techniques reward good reasoning processes. So-called process reward models give the AI feedback on each step of its reasoning, not just the final result, encouraging it to stay on track and catch errors mid-way. This helps models learn self-checking habits instead of blindly forging ahead. As I covered in my blog post , the idea is to have AIs reflect on their chain-of-thought, making them more reliable on complex tasks. Even more exciting, RL is teaching models when and how to call external tools – think code interpreters, web search, calculators, etc. A great example is ReTool , a 2025 framework that blends supervised fine-tuning with RL to train an LLM to interleave natural reasoning with tool usear. The model learns to decide, in the middle of solving a problem, “hey, I should run some code or look up something now,” and then continue with the results. Empirical studies show big gains in multi-hop QA, math problems, and other tasks from this approach. In fact, models begin to demonstrate emergent behaviors such as fixing their own mistakes (e.g. writing code, seeing it fail, then adjusting – an “aha moment” of self-correction) without explicit step-by-step human supervision. It’s basically training the AI to use tools like a pro: not just what to think, but when to reach for a calculator or external knowledge source. This reflective, tool-savvy training is paving the way for more autonomous and competent AI agents. By 2026, expect your AI assistants to be much better at knowing their limits and transparently fetching help (running code, searching the web, etc.) when appropriate. Evolving Agent Architectures (Memory, Multi-Agent & Self-Critique) We’ve also seen rapid development in AI agent architectures – the “brains” that control how models interact in loops or teams. One clear lesson (as I covered my the blog ) is that often a single, well-structured agent is more effective than a kludge of many. Systems like OpenAI’s Codex and Anthropic’s Claude Code run on a lone agent core: essentially a single ReAct-style loop that thinks, calls a tool, observes the result, and repeats. There’s no big swarm of bots chattering; it’s one agent reflecting and iterating until the task is done. This simpler approach is easier to manage and debug, and has become a design pattern for many AI “agentic” applications. That said, we do see multi-agent encapsulation patterns emerging for certain cases. Rather than a free-for-all, these patterns encapsulate specialized sub-agents within a coordinated framework. For example, a coding assistant might have a “writer” agent and a separate “reviewer” agent (a built-in critique buddy). Or a planner agent might delegate subtasks to a solver agent and a verifier agent. The key is isolation and defined roles – each sub-agent has its own memory/context, sometimes even its own prompt, and they communicate via a controlled interface. This avoids the chaos of truly independent agents while still allowing some division of labor. In practice, a lot of “multi-agent” setups are really one master agent prompting a helper agent internally (or using the model to simulate different personas that talk to each other). It’s encapsulated enough that it behaves more like modular components of one system, rather than unpredictable AIs loose in the server. Tools like OpenAI’s Agents SDK support coordinating multiple agents with shared resources, but many implementations simply run agents sequentially (one produces an output, another critiques it, etc.). Memory handling is a huge part of agent architecture now. Long-running agents need to manage conversation history and knowledge without overloading the context window. Claude, for instance, maintains a long-term project memory in a file (e.g. CLAUDE.md) and periodically compacts older dialogue into summaries once the context gets full. This lets it handle very lengthy sessions by retaining key facts in a distilled form. Other agents use vector databases or graphs to store facts they’ve learned, retrieving them as needed. The goal is for the agent to “remember” important details over time without carrying an ever-growing chat log. There’s also interest in critique agents or self-critique loops – basically, having the AI double-check its own outputs. This could mean running a second pass where the model is prompted to find flaws in its last answer and fix them, or using a smaller model to review the bigger model’s work. We’ve seen that even simple reflection prompts (like “Before finalizing, did I make a mistake above?”) can noticeably improve quality. Expect more systems to include a “let me just check my work” phase by default. Overall, agent architectures in 2025 are all about balancing reflective single-agent loops with modular components for memory and oversight. By 2026, your AI coworker might internally be a collection of specialized minds – but it will feel like one coherent assistant that plans, executes, and self-edits smoothly. Standard Toolkits: MCP and Agent SDKs A trend that’s making life easier for developers is the rise of standard protocols and SDKs for agent integration. The standout here is the Model Context Protocol (MCP) – a sort of open standard for connecting AI models to external tools, services, and each other. As I covered in my blog , Anthropic introduced MCP and built it into Claude’s DNA, and now OpenAI has adopted it too for their Codex/Agents frameworks. In plain terms, MCP provides a common language for agents to talk to things like Slack, GitHub, Google Drive, or your internal APIs. Rather than hand-coding every integration, you run an MCP server for a service and the agent can use that service as a tool. This agent SDK integration is becoming the norm. Anthropic’s Claude Agent SDK, for example, is basically the same core that powers Claude’s own assistant, packaged up for custom agent building. It bakes in MCP support, so your custom agent can immediately use a catalog of tools (email, Jira, web browser, etc.) without you writing custom glue code. OpenAI’s Agents SDK similarly lets you orchestrate agents and tools in a standard way. One very cool feature that emerged is dynamic tool discovery. Instead of loading every possible tool into the prompt (which would be huge), Claude can now search a tool repository by description and load tools on the fly. This “Tool Search” means the agent doesn’t need to know upfront about every capability – it can discover new tools as needed (kind of like how a human might say “I need a calculator, do we have one installed?”). This avoids cluttering the context with hundreds of tool definitions and keeps the prompt lean. Another advancement is programmatic tool use: agents can be allowed to write small scripts or API calls as actions, not just use pre-defined tools. Claude’s latest version can generate code that calls multiple tools and processes their results before continuing. This opens up more complex workflows (like gather data from Tool A, transform it, then feed to Tool B, all autonomously). The bottom line is that hooking AI into real-world actions is getting standardized. By 2026, we’ll see robust agent toolkits where adding a new skill to your AI (say, “connect to Salesforce and update records”) is as simple as installing a plugin – the agent can discover it and start using it, securely and effectively. Context Engineering & Long-Term Memory With models sporting bigger context windows a new discipline of context engineering has become critical. It’s not just about prompt wording anymore, but how you structure and manage the context over time. One aspect is optimizing use of the model’s internal KV cache (key-value cache), which stores embeddings of prior tokens to avoid re-processing them each time. Long-term memory is the other side of this coin. Rather than stuffing everything into one gigantic prompt, 2025’s AI systems use external storage and structured memory. For example, an agent might have a vector database of facts it’s learned, or a dedicated memory file it updates (like the CLAUDE.md mentioned earlier). There are patterns emerging – such as storing short-term vs long-term memory separately, tagging memories with metadata (time, importance), and periodically summarizing or archiving old interaction. Another element is personalization through context. By storing user-specific data (safely and with consent, one hopes), models can tailor their output to individual styles or needs. For instance, an AI writing assistant might remember a company’s style guide or a user’s vocabulary preferences and always incorporate those without being reminded. Long-running agent sessions (like AI project managers or game AIs) also benefit from memory – they build up knowledge of the world state and can resume tasks days later. All of this is enabled by a combination of larger base context windows and clever offloading of information to external stores. Going into 2026, we expect “memory” to be a standard part of AI services: your AI will remember who you are, what you’re working on, and the context of prior interactions, making it far more useful and less repetitive. The challenge (and active area of innovation) is how to do this efficiently and securely, ensuring the model recalls the right things at the right time without leaking or misusing stored data. Multimodal Models (Vision-Language Fusion) AI is also breaking out of the text-only box. Multimodal models that integrate vision (and sometimes audio) with language are a huge trend. 2023 saw early examples like GPT-4 Vision, but in 2025 this has accelerated – pretty much every major AI lab has a model that can see or generate images. What’s new is how seamlessly they’re doing it. Instead of separate systems loosely connected, newer models learn a joint latent representation for images and text, meaning they encode different media into the same “brain space” so the model can reason across them. This leads to much better vision-language integration. For example, a visual question answering AI can internally convert an image to embeddings and then use its language reasoning to answer complex questions about the image (“What’s the person doing? Why might they be doing it?”) with far more context understanding. Conversely, image generation models now leverage language models for stronger coherence – they truly understand prompts, not just keywords. A standout is the FLUX series by Black Forest Labs, which in 2025 has pushed the state of the art in image generation. FLUX uses a transformer-based latent diffusion (actually a “latent flow”) architecture that merges text and image processing at a deep level. The results are stunning: FLUX.2 (just released in late 2025) produces 4-megapixel photorealistic images with accurate details and even crisp text rendering (no more gibberish signs in generated images). It outperforms models like DALL·E 3 and Stable Diffusion 3 in clarity and consistency. One key innovation is how it handles the image latent space – basically compressing images into a representation the model can manipulate efficiently, then generating high-res outputs without the usual artifacts. For vision-language tasks, these latent representations mean an AI can hold an understanding of an image in mind while generating fluent text about it (or vice versa, designing an image from a detailed description). We also see multi-reference techniques: FLUX.2 can take several image references plus a prompt, and blend them into a new image that stays consistent with characters or style across scenesllmdevpro.com. That’s huge for things like storyboarding or branding, where consistency is king. Beyond images, “multimodal” encompasses video, audio, and more. There are models now that take video+text or audio+text. But the integration of vision and language is the one going mainstream first. By 2026, it will be expected that AI assistants can analyze an image or generate one on the fly as part of their functionality. We might ask a chatbot to “read” a diagram or screenshot and explain it – and it will. Or ask it to draft an email and create a quick graphic to go with it – and it will. These multimodal agents will use chain-of-thought reasoning that spans modalities (e.g. “First, interpret the chart image; then form the textual answer from that data”). In short, the lines between seeing and reading are blurring for AI. The tech is here; 2026 will be about refining it and weaving it into products. Conclusion: What to Expect in 2026 For a daily pulse on these trends, follow tech.pulsar —a refreshed-daily feed from arXiv, GitHub, and Reddit. Taken together, the AI trends of 2025 point to more capable, more flexible systems that are likely to go mainstream in 2026: Smarter reasoning on demand: We’ll see widespread use of “extended reasoning” modes in AI services (e.g. a toggle for deeper analysis or multiple answer drafts). This means fewer dumb mistakes and more complex problem-solving from AI, at the cost of a bit more time. Power users and enterprise tools will love this for tough tasks. Agents that act and reflect: AI agents will routinely use tools and APIs to get things done – booking events, running calculations, looking up docs – without needing explicit step-by-step user instructions. They’ll also be more self-aware of their reasoning, catching errors with built-in critique steps. This translates to AI that’s more reliable and autonomous, making them better coworkers and assistants. Unified agent platforms: Thanks to standards like MCP and robust SDKs, it will be much easier to integrate AI into existing workflows. In 2026, adding an AI agent to your app (that can connect to your data and tools) could be as straightforward as adding a library. We’ll likely see a boom in domain-specific agents (for finance, law, devops, etc.) built on these common foundations, rather than each reinventing the wheel. Context and personalization as default: Mainstream AI assistants will remember you (in privacy-respecting ways, one hopes). Long-term context will no longer be a neat trick but an expected feature – no more repeating the same info every session. Your preferences, style, and history will travel with your AI across devices. This personalization will make interactions feel more natural and “yours,” increasing adoption in both consumer and enterprise settings. Multimodal everything: By 2026, any top-tier AI will be inherently multimodal. Text-only models will feel limited. Whether it’s customer support bots that can view an uploaded photo of a product issue, or productivity AIs that generate slide deck graphics along with text, the norm will be AI that can seamlessly see, draw, and tell. This opens up a range of new use cases and makes AI far more engaging and useful in everyday life. Overall, the trend is clear: AI systems are becoming more thoughtful, tool-equipped, architecturally robust, context-aware, and versatile across media. Each of these improvements brings us closer to AI that behaves less like a simplistic text generator and more like an actual artificial assistant or collaborator. Mmany of these advancements were in the research or early adopter phase in 2025 – and now we’re on the cusp of them going mainstream. 2026 is shaping up to be the year these AI superpowers (extended reasoning, tool use, long memory, multimodal understanding) really hit products and day-to-day tech. It’s an exciting time to be following AI, and even more exciting to be using it. Stay tuned – and make sure your AI buddy remembers your coffee order, because soon it genuinely will. Community keblezhu 6 days ago 👍 Reply Edit Preview Upload images, audio, and videos by dragging in the text input, pasting, or clicking here . takeaways: Test-time compute scaling lets models allocate extra inference cycles for complex problems, trading latency/cost for better reasoning and fewer errors.
RL and process reward models train agents to reflect on reasoning steps and strategically invoke tools (code, search) when needed, enabling self-correction.
Single well-structured agents with ReAct loops outperform complex multi-agent systems; specialized sub-agents work best when encapsulated with defined roles and isolation.
MCP and agent SDKs standardize tool integration; context engineering and long-term memory become critical for maintaining agent coherence across sessions.
Multimodal models blur vision-language boundaries, enabling richer reasoning loops that combine text, images, and tool outputs in unified agentic pipelines.

title: https://community.openai.com/t/do-you-use-function-calling-or-use-intent-recognition-internal-orchestration/1380376
summary: This community discussion explores design patterns for LLM-based systems, specifically comparing function calling versus intent recognition with internal orchestration. Function calling uses the model directly to invoke external tools via structured outputs, while intent recognition relies on the model to identify user intent and have external logic orchestrate the appropriate actions. The comparison addresses how different architectures trade off simplicity, control, and flexibility—a critical decision for developers building production LLM applications that need reliable tool use and multi-step reasoning.
takeaways: Function calling delegates tool selection to the LLM; intent recognition centralizes orchestration logic outside the model
Function calling may be simpler for straightforward workflows; intent recognition offers tighter control over execution paths
Intent recognition with external orchestration can enforce business logic constraints independent of model behavior
Tool-use architecture choice impacts model latency, error handling, and system observability
Hybrid approaches combining both patterns may optimize for specific application requirements

title: Managed Agents
summary: This article discusses the design and architecture of managed agents within an AI agent harness framework. It explores how to structure agent systems that operate under external control and supervision, addressing challenges in orchestration, state management, and communication protocols. The piece is relevant to practitioners building production AI agent systems who need patterns for reliable, auditable, and scalable agent deployment.
takeaways: Managed agents require explicit control surfaces and lifecycle management beyond standalone agent implementations
State synchronization and persistence mechanisms are critical for reliable multi-step agent workflows
Harness architecture should enforce audit trails and decision logging for transparency and debugging
Communication protocols between agents and harness must handle failures, timeouts, and race conditions gracefully
Resource constraints and concurrency limits must be enforced at the harness level rather than individual agents

title: The Last Harness You'll Ever Build
author: Seong, Haebin, Yin, Li, Zhang, Haoran, Shi, Zhan
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of adapting AI agents to new domain-specific tasks, which typically requires expensive manual harness engineering (designing prompts, tools, orchestration logic, and evaluation criteria). The authors propose a two-level automated framework: the Harness Evolution Loop optimizes a worker agent's configuration for a single task through iterative feedback from an evaluator and evolution agent, while the Meta-Evolution Loop learns a reusable blueprint across diverse tasks that enables rapid convergence on novel domains without human intervention. This approach fundamentally shifts the burden from manual harness design to fully automated harness engineering and meta-learning, potentially enabling practitioners to deploy agents to new workflows with minimal overhead.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2604.21003 (cs) [Submitted on 22 Apr 2026 ( v1 ), last revised 1 May 2026 (this version, v3)] Title: The Last Harness You'll Ever Build Authors: Haebin Seong , Li Yin , Haoran Zhang , Zhan Shi View a PDF of the paper titled The Last Harness You'll Ever Build, by Haebin Seong and 3 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: AI agents are increasingly deployed on complex, domain-specific workflows -- navigating enterprise web applications that require dozens of clicks and form fills, orchestrating multi-step research pipelines that span search, extraction, and synthesis, automating code review across unfamiliar repositories, and handling customer escalations that demand nuanced domain knowledge. \textbf{Each new task domain requires painstaking, expert-driven harness engineering}: designing the prompts, tools, orchestration logic, and evaluation criteria that make a foundation model effective. We present a two-level framework that automates this process. At the first level, the \textbf{Harness Evolution Loop} optimizes a worker agent's harness $\mathcal{H}$ for a single task: a Worker Agent $W_{\mathcal{H}}$ executes the task, an Evaluator Agent $V$ adversarially diagnoses failures and scores performance, and an Evolution Agent $E$ modifies the harness based on the full history of prior attempts. At the second level, the \textbf{Meta-Evolution Loop} optimizes the evolution blueprint $\Lambda = (W_{\mathcal{H}}, \mathcal{H}^{(0)}, V, E)$ itself across diverse tasks, \textbf{learning a blueprint $\Lambda^{(\text{best})}$ that enables rapid harness convergence on any new task -- so that adapting an agent to a novel domain requires no human harness engineering at all.} We formalize the correspondence to meta-learning and present both algorithms. The framework \textbf{shifts manual harness engineering into automated harness engineering}, and takes one step further -- \textbf{automating the design of the automation itself}. takeaways: Two-level framework automates harness design: worker agent executes tasks, evaluator diagnoses failures, evolution agent modifies configuration iteratively.
Meta-evolution loop learns generalizable blueprint across diverse tasks, enabling zero-shot adaptation to novel domains without human engineering.
Shifts burden from manual prompt/tool/orchestration design to fully automated optimization through adversarial evaluation and evolutionary search.
Formalizes correspondence to meta-learning, enabling rapid harness convergence on any new task by learning the automation process itself.

title: CodeVisionary: An Agent-based Framework for Evaluating Large Language Models in Code Generation
author: Wang, Xinchen, Gao, Pengfei, Peng, Chao, Hu, Ruida, Gao, Cuiyun
summary: CodeVisionary is an agent-based evaluation framework addressing limitations of existing LLM code generation evaluation methods. Traditional approaches—human-centered, metric-based, and static LLM-based—struggle with complex, multi-requirement code scenarios and lack fine-grained explainability. CodeVisionary introduces a two-stage pipeline: requirement-guided multi-dimensional context distillation followed by fine-grained scoring and summarization, generating comprehensive evaluation reports. Validated on a new 363-sample benchmark spanning 37 coding scenarios and 23 programming languages, CodeVisionary outperforms baselines by 0.217, 0.163, and 0.141 in Pearson, Spearman, and Kendall-Tau correlations respectively, offering significantly improved explainability for complex code evaluation.
content: Computer Science > Software Engineering arXiv:2504.13472 (cs) [Submitted on 18 Apr 2025 ( v1 ), last revised 20 Oct 2025 (this version, v2)] Title: CodeVisionary: An Agent-based Framework for Evaluating Large Language Models in Code Generation Authors: Xinchen Wang , Pengfei Gao , Chao Peng , Ruida Hu , Cuiyun Gao View a PDF of the paper titled CodeVisionary: An Agent-based Framework for Evaluating Large Language Models in Code Generation, by Xinchen Wang and 4 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in code generation, underscoring the critical need for rigorous and comprehensive evaluation. Existing evaluation approaches fall into three categories, including human-centered, metric-based, and LLM-based. Considering that human-centered approaches are labour-intensive and metric-based ones overly rely on reference answers, LLM-based approaches are gaining increasing attention due to their stronger contextual understanding capabilities. However, they generally evaluate the generated code based on static prompts, and tend to fail for complex code scenarios which typically involve multiple requirements and require more contextual information. In addition, these approaches lack fine-grained evaluation for complex code, resulting in limited explainability. To mitigate the limitations, we propose CodeVisionary, the first agent-based evaluation framework for complex code generation. CodeVisionary consists of two stages: (1) Requirement-guided multi-dimensional context distillation stage and (2) Fine-grained scoring and summarization stage. A comprehensive evaluation report is also generated for enhanced explainability. For validation, we construct a new benchmark consisting of 363 samples spanning 37 coding scenarios and 23 programming languages. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CodeVisionary achieves the best performance among three baselines for evaluating complex code generation, outperforming the best baseline with average improvements of 0.217, 0.163, and 0.141 in Pearson, Spearman, and Kendall-Tau coefficients, respectively. The resources of CodeVisionary are available at this https URL . Subjects: Software Engineering (cs.SE) ; Artificial Intelligence (cs.AI); Computation and Language (cs.CL); Machine Learning (cs.LG) Cite as: arXiv:2504.13472 [cs.SE] (or arXiv:2504.13472v2 [cs.SE] for this version) https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2504.13472 Focus to learn more arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite Submission history From: Chao Peng [ view email ] [v1] Fri, 18 Apr 2025 05:26:32 UTC (964 KB) [v2] Mon, 20 Oct 2025 12:00:10 UTC (758 KB) Full-text links: Access Paper: View a PDF of the paper titled CodeVisionary: An Agent-based Framework for Evaluating Large Language Models in Code Generation, by Xinchen Wang and 4 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) TeX Source view license Current browse context: cs.SE < prev | next > new | recent | 2025-04 Change to browse by: cs cs.AI cs.CL cs.LG References & Citations NASA ADS Google Scholar Semantic Scholar export BibTeX citation Loading... takeaways: Agent-based evaluation framework handles complex code with multiple requirements better than static LLM-based approaches.
Two-stage pipeline: requirement-guided context distillation followed by fine-grained multi-dimensional scoring with explainability.
New benchmark: 363 samples across 37 coding scenarios and 23 programming languages enables rigorous evaluation.
Outperforms baselines by 0.217+ in correlation metrics, demonstrating substantial improvement over existing methods.

title: Agentic Architect: An Agentic AI Framework for Architecture Design Exploration and Optimization
author: Blasberg, Alexander, Kypriotis, Vasilis, Skarlatos, Dimitrios
summary: Agentic Architect presents an LLM-driven framework for automated exploration and optimization of computer architecture designs. The system combines large language models with cycle-accurate simulation to evolve implementations for cache replacement, branch prediction, and data prefetching policies. By specifying optimization targets, seed designs, and scoring functions, human architects guide the LLM's exploration of vast combinatorial microarchitectural spaces. The framework demonstrates competitive or superior results: 1.062x IPC speedup over LRU for cache replacement, 1.100x over Bimodal for branch prediction, and 1.76x over baseline for prefetching. Key insight: evolved designs often recombine known techniques in novel ways, with seed quality and human guidance remaining critical constraints on what agentic search can achieve.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2604.25083 (cs) [Submitted on 28 Apr 2026] Title: Agentic Architect: An Agentic AI Framework for Architecture Design Exploration and Optimization Authors: Alexander Blasberg , Vasilis Kypriotis , Dimitrios Skarlatos View a PDF of the paper titled Agentic Architect: An Agentic AI Framework for Architecture Design Exploration and Optimization, by Alexander Blasberg and 2 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Rapid advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) create new opportunities by enabling efficient exploration of broad, complex design spaces. This is particularly valuable in computer architecture, where performance depends on microarchitectural designs and policies drawn from vast combinatorial spaces. We introduce Agentic Architect, an agentic AI framework for computer architecture design exploration and optimization that combines LLM-driven code evolution with cycle-accurate simulation. The human architect specifies the optimization target, seed design, scoring function, simulator interface, and benchmark split, while the LLM explores implementations within these constraints. Across cache replacement, data prefetching, and branch prediction, Agentic Architect matches or exceeds state-of-the-art designs. Our best evolved cache replacement design achieves a 1.062x geomean IPC speedup over LRU, 0.6% over Mockingjay (1.056x). Our evolved branch predictor achieves a 1.100x geomean IPC speedup over Bimodal, 1.5% over its Hashed Perceptron seed (1.085x). Finally, our evolved prefetcher achieves a 1.76x geomean IPC speedup over no prefetching, 17% over its VA/AMPM Lite seed (1.59x) and 21% over SMS (1.55x). Our analysis surfaces several findings about agentic AI-driven microarchitecture design. Across evolved designs, components often correspond to known techniques; the novelty lies in how they are coordinated. The architect's role is shifting, but the human remains central. Seed quality bounds what search can achieve: evolution can refine and extend an existing mechanism, but cannot compensate for a weak foundation. Likewise, objectives, constraints, and prompt guidance affect reliability and generalization. Overall, Agentic Architect is the first end-to-end open-source framework for agentic AI architecture exploration and optimization. takeaways: LLM-driven architecture exploration matches or exceeds state-of-the-art designs across cache, branch prediction, and prefetching policies.
Evolved designs recombine known techniques rather than discovering fundamentally new mechanisms; novelty emerges from component coordination.
Seed design quality fundamentally bounds optimization outcomes; evolution refines existing mechanisms but cannot overcome weak foundations.
Human architect role shifts but remains central; objectives, constraints, and prompt guidance critically affect reliability and generalization.
First open-source end-to-end framework for agentic AI-driven microarchitecture design and optimization.

title: AI Harness Engineering: A Runtime Substrate for Foundation-Model Software Agents
author: Zhong, Hailin, Zhu, Shengxin
summary: This research paper argues that autonomous software-engineering agents fail not primarily due to model capability gaps, but due to inadequate runtime infrastructure. The authors introduce AI Harness Engineering, a framework that formalizes the runtime substrate mediating how foundation-model agents observe projects, act on them, and receive feedback. They identify eleven critical component responsibilities and propose a four-level progression (H0-H3) that systematically exposes runtime support, along with a trace-based evaluation protocol producing auditable episode packages. The work reframes the central challenge from whether models can generate patches to whether the entire model-harness-environment system produces verifiably correct, attributed, and maintainable changes, outlining a research agenda for essential runtime infrastructure.
content: Computer Science > Software Engineering arXiv:2605.13357 (cs) [Submitted on 13 May 2026] Title: AI Harness Engineering: A Runtime Substrate for Foundation-Model Software Agents Authors: Hailin Zhong , Shengxin Zhu View a PDF of the paper titled AI Harness Engineering: A Runtime Substrate for Foundation-Model Software Agents, by Hailin Zhong and Shengxin Zhu View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Foundation models have transformed automated code generation, yet autonomous software-engineering agents remain unreliable in realistic development settings. The dominant explanation locates this gap in model capability. We propose a different locus: software-engineering capability emerges from a model-harness-environment system, in which a runtime substrate -- the harness -- mediates how a foundation-model agent observes a project, acts on it, receives feedback, and establishes that a change is complete. We formalize this substrate as an AI Harness Engineering and identify eleven component responsibilities: task specification, context selection, tool access, project memory, task state, observability, failure attribution, verification, permissions, entropy auditing, and intervention recording. We operationalize the harness through a four-level ladder (H0-H3) that progressively exposes runtime support to the agent, and we propose a trace-based evaluation protocol that converts each agent run into an auditable episode package. Applied to a controlled validation task, the framework yields episode packages whose evidence structure varies systematically with harness level: lower levels produce only a final patch, higher levels produce reproduction logs, failure attributions, deterministic requirement checks, and structured verification reports. The framework reframes the central question of autonomous software engineering from whether a foundation model can produce a patch to whether the model-harness-environment system can produce a verifiably correct, attributed, and maintainable change. We outline a research program for the runtime systems that foundation-model software agents will require. takeaways: Runtime substrate design, not just model capability, determines autonomous agent reliability in realistic development settings.

Eleven harness components address task specification, context selection, tool access, memory, state, observability, failure attribution, verification, permissions, entropy auditing, and intervention recording.
Four-level harness ladder (H0-H3) progressively exposes runtime support; higher levels produce detailed diagnostics, failure attributions, and structured verification reports.
Trace-based evaluation converts agent runs into auditable episode packages with systematic evidence structures.
Core question shifts from model patch generation to system-level verification, attribution, and maintainability.
